topic: Why Cut Onions Make You Cry (and Garlic Doesn't)

packaging.title: Why Cut Onions Make You Cry (and Garlic Doesn't)
altTitles: The Real Reason Onions Make You Cry
How Onions Fire a Tear-Gas Trap
packaging.description: The food science of why slicing an onion makes you cry, covering the enzyme reaction that builds a volatile tear-gas compound and the simple kitchen tricks that actually shut it down.

thumbnail.visualIdea: a halved onion releasing faint wavy vapor lines rising toward a watering eye

packaging.hookPromise: Your onion isn't just smelly — the moment your knife cuts it, it manufactures an actual airborne tear-gas chemical aimed straight at your eyes. Here's the trap, and how to disarm it.
openLoops: What does the knife actually trigger inside the onion?
Why does chilling the onion stop the tears?
Why doesn't garlic, its close cousin, do this to you?
packaging.payoff: Cutting ruptures cells that mix an enzyme with a sulfur compound, producing a volatile acid gas that turns to mild sulfuric acid on your wet eyes — and because the enzyme works slower when cold and the gas is water-soluble, a chilled onion or a wet cut barely fires.
packaging.whyItWorks: Universal kitchen annoyance with a built-in 'and how do I stop it' payoff that everyone wants.
